title: Getting started with Reva
summary: How the console is organized and the end-to-end flow from intake to export.
content:
# Getting started with Reva

Reva turns inbound reinsurance documents (bordereaux, statements, slips, loss runs) into validated, source-cited data. The flow is: **ingest → classify → extract → reconcile → review → export**.

## The console

- **Workspace** — the cockpit. KPIs (documents, pending review, open exceptions, average confidence), live charts (throughput, confidence distribution, exceptions by type), an upload zone, and the work queue. Drop a PDF, scanned image, Excel/CSV, or `.eml`/`.msg` email to ingest it.
- **Review** — open a document to see the source-cited split view: the parsed source on the left, the extracted fields (with confidence + provenance) on the right, and the control-total reconciliation up top. Edit any field (pencil icon), use **Use expected** on a failing reconciliation row to apply the reconciled value, then **Approve**, **Request changes**, or **Reject**.
- **Export** — turn reviewed data into the formats your market needs (CSV / Excel / JSON), driven by customizable templates.
- **Mappings** — the learned dictionary that maps each sender's column headers to Reva's canonical fields. Override a mapping once and Reva remembers it.
- **Settings** — branding, confidence thresholds, reconciliation tolerance, and the AI model/provider (local Ollama / any OpenAI-compatible server / Hugging Face cloud).
- **Assistant** — a grounded copilot. Ask about any ingested bordereau — fields, classification, exceptions, where a value came from — and it answers from the deterministic engine, citing the document by file name.

## Local-first and source-cited

Reva runs fully offline with a local model, or against cloud inference. Every extracted value carries a citation back to the exact span in the source document, so nothing is a black box.

title: Bordereaux: what they are and the three types
summary: The periodic reports a coverholder submits under a delegated authority, and the Premium / Claims / Risk split.
content:
# Bordereaux explained

A **bordereau** (plural *bordereaux*) is the periodic, detailed report a coverholder, MGA, or ceding insurer submits to the managing agent of a Lloyd's syndicate (or to a reinsurer) documenting the business written under a **binding authority** / delegated authority agreement. The word is French (a memorandum or schedule) and has been used in the Lloyd's market for centuries.

## The three core types

1. **Premium bordereau** — every policy bound during the reporting period: unique risk reference, insured, inception/expiry, sums insured, gross premium, brokerage, taxes, net premium, currency.
2. **Claims bordereau** — claims activity in the period: claim reference, date of loss, date of notification, paid-to-date, outstanding reserve, IBNR, claim status, currency.
3. **Risk bordereau** — risk-level exposure detail (often combined with the premium bordereau).

## Why it matters

Bordereaux are how the syndicate sees, prices, and reserves the delegated book. Late, incomplete, or mis-mapped bordereaux put the binding authority at risk — a delegated authority review, curtailed limits, or termination. Accurate, on-time, standards-compliant reporting is the operational backbone of delegated underwriting.

title: Lloyd's Coverholder Reporting Standards (CRS) v5.2
summary: The mandated column standard for bordereaux, the CR-code data items, and DDM submission.
content:
# Lloyd's Coverholder Reporting Standards v5.2

The **Coverholder Reporting Standards (CRS)** are the data standard Lloyd's mandates for bordereaux. Since **July 2017**, all Lloyd's syndicates must request and collect data consistent with the CRS for risks and claims under any binding authority incepting or renewing from that date. The current core version is **v5.2**.

## Structure

- The standard defines a large set of **data items identified by CR-codes** (e.g. `CR0001`, `CR0013`). The official v5.2 templates carry **~226 columns for the Premium/Risk template and ~150 for the Claims template**, with territory-specific tabs (UK, US, Europe, Hong Kong, Singapore, South Africa, Australia, and others).
- Core premium fields include: Lloyd's **Unique Market Reference (UMR)**, original currency, premium in original currency, exchange rate, premium in settlement currency, coverholder commission rate and amount, net premium to syndicate, policy inception/expiry dates, insured name, risk territory, and class-of-business code.
- Core claims fields add: date of loss, date of notification, claim reference, outstanding reserve, IBNR, paid-to-date, and claim status.

## Submission

Bordereaux are submitted — typically **monthly or quarterly** per the binding agreement — into **DDM (Delegated Data Manager)**, Lloyd's central platform (formerly DA SATS, also known as Tide), where they are validated against the standard.

## How Reva uses it

Reva normalizes any inbound format to its canonical fields, which map to the CRS data items, and exports in CRS-aligned templates so the output is ready for DDM.

title: How Reva reconciles control totals
summary: The deterministic math behind the stated-vs-expected control-total checks and the tolerance.
content:
# How Reva reconciles control totals

Reva validates a bordereau by comparing the **stated** header figures (what the cover note / summary declares) against the **expected** figures it computes deterministically from the attached line items. A mismatch is a genuine, common real-world break — surfaced as a field-level exception, never hidden.

## The method

- For each control total (Premium, Claims, Commission, Net Ceded), **expected = the sum of that column across all line items**. **stated = the value declared in the document header / cover note.**
- **delta = stated − expected.** A check **passes** when `|delta|` is within the configured **reconciliation tolerance** (a percentage of the expected total; set in Settings, default 2.5%). Outside tolerance, it is flagged.
- **Cession %** is checked as `commission` or `net ceded` over `premium`, compared to the stated cession rate, within tolerance.
- Categorical fields (e.g. **Line of Business**) reconcile by normalized string match (so `Prop Cat XL` vs `Property Cat XL` is flagged for review).

## Acting on a break

In Review, each failing row shows `stated → expected` and the delta. **Use expected** applies the reconciled value as a correction to the field; you can also edit any field by hand. Approving records your decision; the exceptions remain visible for audit.

Every figure and agreement score is **computed from the document's own data — never hardcoded**.

title: Confidence tiers and document classification
summary: How Reva scores extraction confidence and classifies document types.
content:
# Confidence tiers and document classification

## Confidence tiers

Each extracted field carries a confidence score in [0, 1], rendered as a tier:

- **High** — at or above the medium ceiling (default 0.85).
- **Medium** — between the low and medium ceilings (default 0.6 – 0.85).
- **Low** — below the low ceiling (default 0.6).

The thresholds are configurable in **Settings → Confidence thresholds**. Confidence reflects the strength of the source evidence (a clean digital table scores higher than a sparse OCR scan).

## Document classification

Reva classifies each document into a reinsurance type: **Bordereau, Statement of Account, Facultative Slip, Loss Run, Treaty, Endorsement, Claim Notice**, or **Unclassified** (Unknown) when the content doesn't match a known shape (e.g. a blank template or a non-reinsurance note). Classification is content-based, so a Premium bordereau in Excel, CSV, PDF, an emailed CSV attachment, or a scanned image all resolve to **Bordereau**.

Unclassified documents are still ingested (best-effort, low confidence) and never rejected — you can correct the type in Review.

title: Edge cases Reva handles
summary: Multi-currency, adjustments, negative premiums, missing fields, OCR, and file formats.
content:
# Edge cases Reva handles

Real bordereaux are messy. Reva is built for the cases that break manual spreadsheets:

- **Multi-currency & FX** — original vs settlement currency and exchange rates are preserved; control totals reconcile within the same currency.
- **Mid-term endorsements & adjustments** — additional or return premium lines net correctly into the period totals.
- **Negative premiums (cancellations)** — negative line items are summed faithfully; the delta math handles signs.
- **Claims movements** — paid vs outstanding vs IBNR are distinct fields, not a single number.
- **Missing fields** — a field not found in the source is marked *missing* (not zero) and is editable so a reviewer can supply it; it never silently defaults.
- **OCR & scans** — image and scanned-PDF bordereaux run through OCR; low-confidence regions are flagged rather than trusted blindly.
- **Any file format** — PDF, Excel (.xlsx/.xls/.ods), CSV/TSV, Word/PowerPoint, images (PNG/JPG/TIFF), and email (.eml/.msg with attachments parsed recursively). Unknown formats fall back to best-effort text extraction.
- **Stated vs line-item disagreement** — the most common real break: declared totals that don't match the detail. Reva surfaces it instead of papering over it.
- **Period boundaries & duplicates** — documents are tracked by content hash so the same bordereau isn't double-counted.
